topic: Rust/Tauri vs Electron for a Kanban board desktop application — which framework, and under what conditions?

content.position: Electron for this Kanban app: the drag-heavy board, cards/columns, and real-time-ish sync are exactly where the mature JS ecosystem shines — dnd-kit handles accessible, multi-column drag out of the box, and you reuse the same React/TS the web app already uses, hitting the launch window in weeks not quarters.
content.conditions: Team is JS/TS-native with little/no Rust experience; aggressive launch deadline; product is feature/UX-differentiated rather than resource-constrained; users are on reasonably modern laptops where ~150MB install and ~200-400MB RAM is acceptable; you want one codebase shared with a web/PWA version.
content.wouldChangeIf: A hard requirement for <10MB installers or low-RAM/older hardware; the team already has Rust fluency; security/supply-chain review forbids shipping bundled Chromium; or a Tauri+dnd-kit spike proves the same drag UX ships in comparable time with the webview's quirks (drag ghosting, OS-specific DnD) fully handled.
content.keyRisk: Per-user resource cost and install size balloon (multiple Electron apps on one machine), plus ongoing burden of tracking Chromium security patches and auto-update bandwidth — costs I tend to discount but that compound across a large non-technical install base.

content.position: For a drag-heavy Kanban app distributed to non-technical users cross-platform, I'd pick Tauri — the runtime footprint (typically 5-15MB installer, ~80-150MB RAM idle vs Electron's 80-150MB installer, 250-400MB RAM) and reduced attack surface directly serve a tool people leave open all day on modest hardware.
content.conditions: Holds if the team has (or will build) Rust competence, the UI is React/Svelte/Vanilla in the webview (not a native widget rewrite), real-time sync is light (websocket/polling, not heavy native compute), and Linux support can target a known WebKitGTK baseline rather than 'every distro perfectly'.
content.wouldChangeIf: I'd flip to Electron if: (a) WebKitGTK drag-and-drop / pointer-event / CSS inconsistencies on Linux materially break the board UX in testing, (b) the team needs a mature library (e.g. specific DnD, charting, or offline-sync libs) that assumes Chromium quirks, or (c) ship-date pressure makes a single guaranteed-consistent Chromium engine worth the RAM tax.
content.keyRisk: WebView2/WKWebView/WebKitGTK rendering and input-event divergence — a buttery drag-and-drop board on Chromium may stutter or misbehave on Linux WebKitGTK, and chasing per-engine fixes can erase the engineering time saved by the smaller binary.

content.framing: The two positions share goals (a usable, maintainable, accessible Kanban board) and diverge less on values than on one empirical unknown and one risk-weighting. The real fault line is: does system-webview rendering — specifically WebKitGTK on Linux — degrade multi-column drag-and-drop enough to erase Tauri's footprint/security savings in re-engineering time? Tellingly, BOTH named that exact question as their flip condition, so this is a testable bet, not a clash of principles. The secondary, genuine disagreement is how to price resource/security cost (per-user RAM + Chromium patch/auto-update burden across the install base) against time-to-launch under the team's existing JS fluency — i.e. who absorbs which budget.

questions.question: Both of you flagged WebKitGTK drag-and-drop as your flip condition, so it must be measured, not asserted. Define the spike concretely: which dnd-kit (or alternative) DnD behaviors do you predict break or diverge on WebKitGTK vs WebView2/WKWebView (pointer capture, drag-image/ghost rendering, autoscroll, keyboard-driven reorder for accessibility), what pass/fail thresholds end the spike, and how many engineer-days does it take before you'd commit to Tauri?

Your keyRisk is the Chromium security-patch and auto-update burden across a large install base — yet you'd still ship Electron. Put numbers on it: at what install-base size and update cadence does that ongoing cost (CVE-driven re-bundles, forced auto-updates, ~150MB delta downloads) overtake the weeks you save at launch, and what concrete accessibility or DnD capability in dnd-kit are you betting no Tauri+system-webview spike can match within that same launch window?

Name the single most likely real failure case that would make you abandon Tauri mid-build (not 'rendering divergence' in the abstract) — e.g. a specific WKWebView/WebView2 input-event or CSS behavior — and state what you'd do if it surfaces in week 3: patch around it, swap the webview baseline, or concede the Chromium-uniformity argument?

disagreementBudget.rationale: Both experts declared 0.60 confidence and overlapping flip conditions, so the gap is narrow and resolvable by evidence rather than persuasion — pushing toward the lower end. But a target below 3 would let them collapse into 'just run a spike' and skip the genuine, non-empirical disagreement about how to weight per-user resource/security cost against launch velocity. A target of 4 keeps enough friction to force concrete spike criteria and explicit cost thresholds, while signaling this should converge once the WebKitGTK DnD question is answered.

content.position: Hold Electron at conf 0.58 for the v1 launch, but I now treat this as a near-coin-flip on a 12-24 month horizon: ship Chromium+dnd-kit to hit the window, and fund a parallel Tauri spike whose result is a real flip trigger, not a formality.
content.reasoning: Steel-manning r1-msg-002: Tauri's 5-15MB installer vs Electron's ~85-120MB, and ~80-150MB RAM vs 250-400MB, are real and durable; for an always-open daily board on modest hardware the 150-250MB RAM delta is the single most defensible point against me, and the reduced attack surface is genuine. Honest correction on r1-msg-003's numbers: Electron auto-update (electron-updater + nsis/Squirrel) is DELTA/differential, so a routine Chromium bump is ~5-25MB/user not 150MB — that cuts against my own keyRisk. Napkin math: Chromium ~8-12 security releases/yr; re-bundle the Electron major ~4-8x/yr at ~4-8 eng-hrs each => ~24-64 eng-hrs/yr (~1-1.5 eng-weeks/yr). Bandwidth at 50k installs x 6 updates x 15MB = ~4.5TB/yr egress = a few hundred USD = negligible. So crossover isn't bandwidth; it's eng-attention + per-user RAM: the flip lands around 100k-250k always-open DAU on low-RAM machines, OR ~50k installs the moment a Chromium 0-day forces an emergency out-of-band re-bundle under SLA. The dnd-kit bet: I wager a Tauri+WebKitGTK spike can't cheaply match dnd-kit's accessible KeyboardSensor across a multi-column board on Linux — live ARIA announcements, roving focus, keyboard pickup/move/drop that survive virtualized columns (WebKitGTK ATK/AT-SPI path). What it PROBABLY CAN match: basic pointer drag, collision detection (plain JS), auto-scroll.
proposals: Ship Electron for v1 to hit the launch window; stay within one Chromium major using electron-updater delta updates.

Time-box a 1-week Tauri+dnd-kit spike NOW targeting ONLY the hard case: accessible KeyboardSensor multi-column DnD with live ARIA announcements on WebKitGTK, plus pointer-drag on a virtualized 1k-row column.
Define the flip as binary: spike shows a11y + keyboard drag parity on WebKitGTK with <1 eng-week glue => concede, go Tauri.
Instrument post-launch telemetry (DAU, session length, resident RAM, install base) so the 50k/100k crossover is measured.
Pre-write the emergency re-bundle runbook so a Chromium 0-day costs hours, de-risking my own keyRisk.
counterpoints: Delta auto-updates gut my own bandwidth argument, so 'CVE re-bundle burden' is ~1.5 eng-weeks/yr — small enough that runtime-architect can fairly call it a rounding error.
dnd-kit renders to standard DOM/CSS/ARIA; WebKitGTK may already handle the KeyboardSensor path fine, in which case my single hardest bet collapses and Tauri wins outright.
I systematically discount per-user RAM; 250-400MB across 100k+ always-open users is a real aggregate cost.
If the team has any Rust fluency, my 'unmaintainable Rust' fear is overweighted.
questions: Can you produce a WebKitGTK/Linux recording of accessible KEYBOARD drag-and-drop across multiple columns with live screen-reader announcements (not pointer-only), and the eng-hours of glue it took — that single artifact decides this for me?
Under a Chromium 0-day with a 72h compliance SLA, what's Tauri's equivalent exposure when the bug lives in the user's system WebKitGTK/WebView2 that you DON'T control and can't force-patch?
content.wouldChangeIf: A 1-week Tauri+dnd-kit spike demonstrates accessible KeyboardSensor multi-column DnD with live ARIA announcements on WebKitGTK AND smooth pointer-drag over a virtualized 1k-row column, under ~1 eng-week of glue — then I flip to Tauri for v1.

content.position: Hold Tauri+system-webview at conf 0.60 as the default, but gate it on a hard 5-eng-day WebKitGTK DnD spike: ship lean/native unless that spike fails parity, in which case I concede to Electron rather than burn weeks fighting WebKitGTK.
content.reasoning: Steel-manning r1-msg-001: dnd-kit gives accessible, keyboard-operable multi-column reorder out of the box, and reusing a React/TS team means features in weeks not a Rust ramp — that velocity is real. Counter: Electron ships ~250-400MB RAM and a bundled Chromium on a CVE treadmill (V8/Skia/sqlite advisories monthly) vs Tauri's ~80-150MB RAM and 5-15MB installer riding the OS-patched webview. KEY technical point on the WebKitGTK fault line: dnd-kit deliberately AVOIDS HTML5 drag-and-drop — it uses setPointerCapture + transforms (pointer/keyboard sensors) — so the classic WebKitGTK HTML5 drag-image bug is largely MOOT. The real WebKitGTK 2.4x risks are perf/polish, not capability: (1) pointer event coalescing/latency making fast-drag autoscroll jittery, (2) setPointerCapture edge cases on rapid pointerdown->move, (3) compositing/will-change layer promotion making the overlay janky or position:sticky headers detach, (4) transform repaint cost on large lists. These are measurable, which is why a spike resolves it.
proposals: SPIKE PLAN (5 eng-days, answers Q1): Day1-2 build a 3-column 200-card dnd-kit board in a Tauri shell across WebView2(Win)/WKWebView(mac)/WebKitGTK 2.40+(Ubuntu 22.04). Measure 4 behaviors with explicit pass/fail: (a) DragOverlay ghost tracks pointer within 1 frame, no flicker [FAIL if >2-frame lag/blank]; (b) setPointerCapture continuity 0 dropped drags over 50 rapid cancels [FAIL on any lost capture]; (c) autoscroll velocity monotonic at 60fps [FAIL if <30fps stutter]; (d) keyboard reorder (Space-grab/arrows/Esc) + SR live-region identical on Orca vs NVDA vs VoiceOver [FAIL on missing announcements]. Day3 stress 1000 cards + sticky headers + will-change. Day4 fix-or-fail triage. Day5 go/no-go.
Commit threshold: >=3 of 4 PASS on WebKitGTK with <=1 day CSS workaround each => commit Tauri. 2+ hard FAIL => concede Electron, no multi-week yak-shave.

Q2 single most likely failure + week-3 contingency: THE failure is autoscroll jank on WebKitGTK during edge-drag of a long virtualized list (pointermove coalescing + compositor scheduling). Week 3: first patch around it (manual fixed-step scroll loop + single promoted overlay layer, ~1-2 days); if still <50fps I do NOT swap the webview baseline mid-build — I concede Chromium-uniformity for the LINUX target specifically and ship Tauri on Win/mac + Electron/CEF on Linux, OR concede fully to Electron if a split build is unacceptable.
Pin WebKitGTK >=2.40 as documented min requirement; budget the spike BEFORE production feature code.
counterpoints: Blind-spot concession: Electron's Chromium uniformity genuinely saves time on ecosystem edges — virtualized data-grid + sticky headers + drag, offline-sync stacks, charting libs (consistent Skia/canvas + IndexedDB/Service Worker) behave identically across OSes, whereas Tauri pays per-platform QA for WebKitGTK canvas/IndexedDB quirks. Real recurring cost I underweight.
Even if the spike passes, ongoing per-release WebKit regressions on Linux could nickel-and-dime velocity in ways the spike won't catch.
Zero Rust fluency could eat the RAM/security savings via slower iteration on every non-DnD feature.
questions: To velocity-lead: if a 5-day spike shows dnd-kit hits 60fps drag + autoscroll and identical keyboard/SR reorder on WebKitGTK 2.40+, does the RAM + monthly Chromium-CVE burden flip you, or is there a velocity cost beyond DnD (charting/offline-sync) you'd still cite to stay on Electron?
To moderator: is a split build (Tauri on Win/mac, Electron/CEF on Linux) in-scope as a partial outcome, or must one stack win all three platforms?
content.wouldChangeIf: I flip to Electron if the spike shows >=2 of 4 behaviors FAIL hard on WebKitGTK 2.40+ (esp. autoscroll <30fps or lost pointer-capture) with no <=1-day workaround, OR if the product has a hard pixel-exact cross-platform rendering requirement (canvas/charting/data-grid) that makes WebKitGTK per-platform QA cost more than the RAM/security savings, OR if there's no hard low-RAM/footprint requirement at all.

content.consensusTargeted: Both experts (r1-msg-004, r1-msg-005) agree the decision reduces to an empirical question settled by a short, instrumented WebKitGTK drag-and-drop spike with numeric pass/fail thresholds, with footprint favoring Tauri and ecosystem/velocity favoring Electron.
content.loadBearingAssumption: That DnD on WebKitGTK is the dominant, decision-determining risk of choosing Tauri — i.e., a 5-day spike on 3-4 DnD behaviors is a sufficient statistic for the framework choice, and the rest (Rust/CI/signing plumbing, the long tail of webview divergences, product/team factors) is either cheap, knowable, or irrelevant.
content.weakestEvidence: r1-msg-005's claim that the spike's 4 pass/fail DnD behaviors 'decide' Tauri vs Electron. It is weakest because dnd-kit's own architecture (setPointerCapture + CSS transforms) makes DnD the LEAST likely WebKitGTK failure mode — yet the experts designate exactly that low-risk area as the deciding test, while the genuinely recurring tax (IME/CJK, native file dialogs, drag-OUT to OS, printing/PDF, codecs, IndexedDB/SW quirks, WebKitGTK version skew across distros) is never instrumented and cannot surface in 5 days.
content.failureScenario: The spike passes all 4 DnD thresholds, so the team picks Tauri per r1-msg-005. Three months later they hit the untested long tail: CJK IME composition drops characters in card editors on WebKitGTK 2.40 but not 2.44 (distro skew), the native file-attachment dialog and drag-a-card-out-to-the-OS behave differently per platform, and PDF/print export of a board is broken. None was in the spike. The recurring fix cost dwarfs the RAM savings that justified Tauri (r1-msg-002), and the team is mid-migration with sunk Rust/signing/notarization plumbing — strictly worse than treating a passing spike as necessary-but-not-sufficient.
content.amplifiedMinority: Velocity-lead's own r1-msg-004 admission that this is a 'near coin-flip' and that the maintenance-vs-velocity crossover only bites at 100k-250k always-open DAU or ~50k installs under a 0-day SLA. Combined with both experts at 0.58-0.60 recommending OPPOSITE frameworks, the honest read is that the shell barely moves the needle versus team Rust competence, hiring, and the Kanban category's real differentiator (sync/collaboration) — and a DnD spike is motivated reasoning that lets both defer admitting the choice is low-stakes.
content.whatWouldProveMeWrong: Show that (1) the long tail is bounded and cheap on Tauri v2 — IME/CJK, file dialogs, drag-out, print/PDF, codecs, IndexedDB/SW, version skew are non-issues for a Kanban app or covered by stable plugins; (2) the Rust/CI/signing/notarization/auto-update plumbing is a one-time well-trodden cost absorbed without ongoing tax; (3) the framework materially affects product success (users skew low-RAM or security-gated) so the choice is genuinely high-stakes. If all three hold, a passing DnD spike plus known footprint really is close to a sufficient statistic.

content.position: Hold Electron at conf 0.58. The Contrarian's long-tail argument cuts in MY favor — Chromium uniformity is precisely what eliminates the non-DnD divergence tax (IME/CJK, file dialogs, codecs, Service Worker quirks, distro version skew). I concede the framework choice is lower-stakes than team Rust-competence and the category's real differentiator (sync/collaboration); those should drive the call, and on those grounds Electron still wins by reducing total surface area.
content.reasoning: Steel-manning r1-msg-006: a 5-day DnD spike is a biased estimator — it samples the path runtime-architect already proved safe (dnd-kit's setPointerCapture avoids HTML5 drag per r1-msg-005), so passing it says nothing about the unsampled long tail, and 'team builds Rust competence' silently capitalizes CI/signing/notarization/auto-update plumbing + hiring as zero. Correct, and it damages the SPIKE as a decision instrument. But the honest twist: every long-tail item — WebKitGTK IME/CJK, file dialogs, drag-OUT, print/PDF, codecs, IndexedDB/SW skew across distros — is a per-engine-divergence cost Electron pays ONCE against bundled Chromium and ships identically on every distro. The long-tail argument indicts the engine that VARIES (WebKitGTK), not Electron. So r1-msg-006 strengthens my case more than it wounds it. On the coin-flip point I agree: two experts at 0.58-0.60 recommending opposites means the shell delta is small vs team/product factors. What SHOULD drive it: (1) does the team have shippable Rust/CI/notarization competence TODAY — if no, Electron removes a launch-risk dependency; (2) is the moat sync/collaboration — if yes, spend saved eng-weeks there, not on cross-engine QA.
content.previousPosition: Electron conf 0.58, near coin-flip, last-stand on dnd-kit accessible KeyboardSensor + live ARIA being hard to match on WebKitGTK.
content.currentPosition: Electron conf 0.58, reasoning re-grounded: decisive factor is engine-variance/team-competence not DnD; framework conceded lower-stakes than sync moat and Rust readiness, both of which still favor Electron at this scale.
content.shiftReason: r1-msg-006 did not move confidence because its central long-tail mechanism (cross-engine divergence) is an argument FOR Chromium uniformity; it sharpened/broadened my rationale rather than weakening it.

content.position: I still prefer Tauri on resource/security merits but drop confidence 0.60 -> ~0.54 and re-scope the bet: the deciding variable is NOT DnD (covered by my spike, de-risked by dnd-kit's pointer path) but the WebKitGTK non-DnD long tail, which a 5-day spike cannot bound. I now agree the shell choice is near coin-flip and likely subordinate to sync/collaboration architecture.
content.reasoning: Steel-man of r1-msg-006, conceded: my spike measures the path I already believe is safe, so it is NOT a sufficient statistic. Honest split — BOUNDED by stable Tauri v2 plugins/OS APIs: native file dialogs (plugin-dialog), notifications (plugin-notification), auto-update + signing/notarization (plugin-updater + tauri-action CI), and IME/CJK (handled by the OS-native webview text stack — actually a Tauri STRENGTH vs bundled Chromium). GENUINELY UNBOUNDED, conceded to the Contrarian: (a) WebKitGTK version skew across distros (we don't pin the engine); (b) print/PDF export fidelity (WebKitGTK print path is weaker, unswappable); (c) codec/media (gated by system GStreamer); (d) drag-OUT to OS + SW/IndexedDB eviction partially covered, not claimed solved. On Rust plumbing: conceded — CI cross-compilation, signing/notarization, updater key management, hiring are real upfront cost Electron mostly gives you via Forge. Pushback on r1-msg-006: it frames ALL long-tail as unbounded; file dialogs/notifications/updater/IME are bounded by shipped v2 plugins, so the unbounded set is narrower than stated — but nonzero and unkillable by a spike, which is the point that moves me.
content.previousPosition: Tauri at conf 0.60, gated on a 5-eng-day WebKitGTK DnD spike, arguing dnd-kit's pointer path makes the classic drag bug moot.
content.currentPosition: Tauri at ~0.54, near coin-flip; BROADEN the spike to add a long-tail probe pass (print/PDF export, media/codec check, WebKitGTK version-skew matrix across 2-3 distro versions). Accept unbounded items (engine skew, print, codecs) as genuine Tauri risk; agree shell is likely lower-stakes than sync/collaboration architecture.
content.shiftReason: r1-msg-006's selection-bias argument is correct: my spike validated the path I already trusted, so it couldn't surface the recurring non-DnD WebKitGTK tax. That plus the conceded Rust CI/signing/notarization plumbing cost legitimately lowers my confidence and reframes the spike scope — though file dialogs/notifications/updater/IME being bounded by stable v2 plugins keeps me from conceding fully.

positionShifts.from: Tauri @ conf 0.60, gated on a 5-eng-day WebKitGTK DnD spike (dnd-kit pointer path makes the classic drag bug moot).
positionShifts.to: Tauri @ ~0.54 (near coin-flip); BROADEN the spike to a long-tail probe pass (print/PDF, codec/media, WebKitGTK version-skew matrix); concede engine-skew/print/codecs as genuinely unbounded Tauri risk and the shell as lower-stakes than sync/collaboration architecture.
positionShifts.reasoning: Accepted the Contrarian's spike-selection-bias argument (the DnD spike validated the already-safe path) plus the conceded Rust CI/signing/notarization plumbing cost; velocity-lead's r1-msg-004 'near coin-flip' framing pulled confidence toward parity. Held short of full concession because file dialogs/notifications/updater/IME are bounded by stable Tauri v2 plugins.

qualityScore.notes: genuineDisagreement: real fault line on WebKitGTK long-tail tax vs Chromium uniformity, but both near coin-flip; evidenceQuality: concrete numbers (RAM/installer/maint-weeks/DAU crossover) + falsifiable spike design, though some figures asserted not cited; steelManning: contrarian and both experts engaged each other's strongest forms, conceded selection bias and Rust plumbing; novelInsights: dnd-kit-moots-classic-drag-bug and bounded-vs-unbounded long-tail split are genuinely reframing; positionEvolution: only one minor 0.06 shift, velocity-lead held firm (re-grounded not moved); overall: high-quality round that nearly converged on a reframe.

synthesis.summary: Tight round: experts started 0.60/0.60 on opposite picks, ended ~0.58 Electron vs ~0.54 Tauri. Contrarian reframed the real cost as the non-DnD WebKitGTK long tail, not the (mooted) classic drag bug. Both conceded shell choice is lower-stakes than sync/collab moat and team Rust-readiness.

agreements.point: dnd-kit uses pointer capture + transforms, not HTML5 drag, so the classic WebKitGTK drag bug is moot for the core Kanban interaction
The shell choice is lower-stakes than the real differentiators (sync/collaboration moat, team Rust-readiness)
Some long-tail items (file dialogs, notifications, updater, IME via OS webview) are bounded and a Tauri strength; the WebKitGTK risk concentrates in version skew, print/PDF, codecs
A scoped WebKitGTK spike is the right de-risking instrument, but must be broadened beyond DnD to be a sufficient statistic

activeDisagreements.point: Does the non-DnD WebKitGTK long tail (version skew/print-PDF/codecs/CJK-IME) impose a recurring tax that outweighs Tauri's footprint wins, or does Chromium uniformity pay that cost once and amortize it?

positions.stance: WebKitGTK long-tail divergence is the genuinely unbounded recurring tax; Chromium uniformity wins
Long tail splits into bounded (OS-webview strengths) vs a narrower unbounded set worth a broadened spike before deciding

activeDisagreements.point: Primary decision basis: ship velocity + React/TS reuse now vs runtime footprint (5-15MB / 80-150MB RAM) and reduced attack surface

positions.stance: Velocity and stack reuse dominate; maintenance crossover is far off (~100k-250k DAU / ~50k installs)
Footprint and security justify the engineering cost for the target profile

activeDisagreements.point: Hidden Rust CI/signing/notarization plumbing cost — material drag or one-time setup?

insights.insight: dnd-kit avoids HTML5 drag (setPointerCapture + transforms), so the headline WebKitGTK drag-degradation fault line is moot — the debate must move off the safest path
The DnD spike measures the SAFEST path, so it is not a sufficient statistic; the real recurring tax is the non-DnD long tail plus hidden Rust signing/notarization plumbing
Splitting the long tail into BOUNDED (file dialogs/notifications/updater/IME via OS webview — a Tauri strength) vs genuinely UNBOUNDED (version skew/print-PDF/codecs) localizes the real risk
The long-tail critique can cut FOR Chromium: Electron pays per-engine divergence once, whereas WebKitGTK is the engine that varies across distros
Quantified maintenance-vs-velocity crossover (~100k-250k always-open DAU or ~50k installs under a Chromium 0-day SLA) plus delta-updates making bandwidth negligible (~1.5 eng-weeks/yr)

openQuestions: What is the projected scale (DAU / install count) and is it near the ~100k-250k DAU / ~50k-install crossover?
What does a broadened WebKitGTK spike covering version skew, print/PDF, codecs, and CJK/IME actually measure pass/fail?
Is the team Rust-ready, and what is the true one-time cost of Rust CI/signing/notarization plumbing?
Is Linux a first-class target at all? If not, the WebKitGTK long tail largely evaporates and the calculus shifts to Tauri.
How load-bearing is the sync/collaboration moat, and is it actually shell-independent as claimed?
synthesis.recommendationReason: Convergence is genuine, not premature: the round dissolved the headline fault line (drag bug mooted), localized residual risk to a narrow unbounded set, and surfaced that the true deciders (scale vs crossover, Linux-target, Rust-readiness, sync moat) are scope/context inputs the experts cannot resolve by further debate. A second round would re-litigate the same coin-flip.
